title: Spring Security Expert Assessment
description: Expert-level evaluation focusing on Spring Security architecture, authentication providers, OAuth2/OIDC, JWT handling, method-level security, security filter chain, CORS/CSRF protection, session management, and common security vulnerabilities (OWASP).

questions.text: What is the correct order of filters in Spring Security's default filter chain?
questions.explanation: Standard order: SecurityContextPersistenceFilter → UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter → BasicAuthenticationFilter → ExceptionTranslationFilter → AuthorizationFilter (formerly FilterSecurityInterceptor).

questions.text: Which interface must you implement to create a custom authentication mechanism in Spring Security?
questions.explanation: AuthenticationProvider interface with authenticate() and supports() methods. It processes authentication requests and returns fully populated Authentication object.

questions.text: What is the difference between UserDetailsService and AuthenticationProvider?
options: UserDetailsService loads user data by username; AuthenticationProvider performs actual authentication logic (password verification, etc.)
questions.explanation: UserDetailsService is a DAO interface for retrieving user data. AuthenticationProvider validates credentials and can delegate to UserDetailsService. DaoAuthenticationProvider combines both.

questions.text: How do you configure Spring Security to use a custom password encoder that wraps BCrypt with a pepper?
options: Extend BCryptPasswordEncoder and override encode() method
Create a DelegatingPasswordEncoder with BCrypt as default
Implement PasswordEncoder interface and combine BCrypt with pepper (secret added to password before hashing)
questions.explanation: Implement PasswordEncoder interface. A pepper (application-wide secret) is added to the password before hashing. Store pepper in environment variable, not in code or database.

questions.text: What is the purpose of SecurityContextHolder in Spring Security?
options: To hold the current security context (Authentication object) for the current thread
questions.explanation: SecurityContextHolder stores SecurityContext (containing Authentication) using ThreadLocal. Strategy can be MODE_THREADLOCAL, MODE_INHERITABLETHREADLOCAL, or MODE_GLOBAL.

questions.text: How do you propagate SecurityContext to child threads in async processing?
options: Cannot propagate security context to threads
questions.explanation: Set MODE_INHERITABLETHREADLOCAL or use DelegatingSecurityContextRunnable/TaskDecorator. Spring's @Async with proper configuration wraps tasks with security context.

questions.text: What is the correct way to implement stateless JWT authentication in Spring Security?
options: Configure session management as STATELESS and add custom JwtAuthenticationFilter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter
questions.explanation: Set sessionManagement().sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS). Add custom OncePerRequestFilter that extracts JWT from Authorization header and sets Authentication in SecurityContext.

questions.text: How do you handle JWT revocation (logout) in a stateless architecture?
options: Maintain a denylist (blacklist) of revoked JWTs in Redis until token expiration
questions.explanation: Maintain a denylist of JWT IDs (jti) or token hashes in fast storage (Redis). On each request, check if token is revoked. Use short-lived tokens with refresh tokens.

questions.text: What does @PreAuthorize("hasPermission(#id, 'document', 'read')") do?
options: It checks if user has role ADMIN
It uses a custom PermissionEvaluator to check permission on a domain object
questions.explanation: hasPermission() calls a custom PermissionEvaluator with targetId (#id), targetType ('document'), and permission ('read'). Used for fine-grained ACL-style authorization.

questions.text: How do you enable method-level security with @PreAuthorize in Spring Security?
options: Add @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true) (pre Spring Security 6) or @EnableMethodSecurity (Spring Security 6+)
questions.explanation: Spring Security 6+ uses @EnableMethodSecurity. Older versions used @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled=true). Without these, @PreAuthorize/@PostAuthorize are ignored.

questions.text: What is the difference between @PreAuthorize and @PostAuthorize?
options: @PreAuthorize checks before method execution; @PostAuthorize checks after method execution (using returnObject)
@PreAuthorize is for service layer; @PostAuthorize for controllers
questions.explanation: @PreAuthorize prevents method entry if condition fails. @PostAuthorize executes method but throws AccessDeniedException if condition fails. Useful for checking ownership based on return value.

questions.text: How does Spring Security protect against CSRF attacks by default?
options: By generating a CSRF token and requiring it in state-changing requests (POST, PUT, DELETE)
CSRF protection is disabled by default
questions.explanation: CSRF protection is enabled by default. Spring Security generates a token stored in HttpSession or cookie. State-changing requests must include token as header (X-XSRF-TOKEN) or form parameter.

questions.text: When should you disable CSRF protection in Spring Security?
options: Only for stateless REST APIs where clients (mobile/SPA) don't use browser cookies for authentication (e.g., JWT in Authorization header)
questions.explanation: Disable CSRF for stateless REST APIs where authentication uses Authorization header (Bearer token) instead of cookies. Browser-based apps with sessions should keep CSRF enabled.

questions.text: What is the correct CORS configuration for a REST API that serves a SPA on localhost:4200?
options: CORS is not needed for localhost
questions.explanation: Specific origin (http://localhost:4200) is correct. Allowing all origins (*) with credentials is unsafe and not allowed. Use @CrossOrigin on controllers or configure globally.

questions.text: How does Spring Security prevent Session Fixation attacks?
options: By creating a new session ID on authentication (session fixation protection)
questions.explanation: SessionFixationProtectionStrategy (default) creates a new session ID or migrates session attributes to a new session on authentication, preventing attacker from fixing session ID.

questions.text: What is the purpose of SecurityContextRepository?
options: To load and save SecurityContext between requests (e.g., HttpSessionSecurityContextRepository for session, RequestAttributeSecurityContextRepository for stateless)
questions.explanation: SecurityContextRepository loads SecurityContext before filter chain and saves it after. Default is HttpSessionSecurityContextRepository. For stateless JWT, use custom implementation or NullSecurityContextRepository.

questions.text: How do you integrate OAuth2 Resource Server with JWT in Spring Boot?
options: Add spring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-server and configure jwt issuer URI or public key
Manually validate JWT in each controller
questions.explanation: Add spring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-server. Configure spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.issuer-uri or jwk-set-uri. Spring Security automatically validates JWT and extracts authentication.

questions.text: What is the difference between authorization_code and client_credentials OAuth2 grant types?
options: authorization_code is for user delegation (user logs in); client_credentials is for machine-to-machine (no user)
questions.explanation: Authorization code grant involves user authentication and consent, returning code exchanged for token. Client credentials uses only client ID/secret for server-to-server communication without user context.

questions.text: How do you extract user roles from a JWT claim (e.g., 'roles' claim) in Spring Security?
options: Parse JWT manually in each controller
Implement JwtAuthenticationConverter and map claims to GrantedAuthority
questions.explanation: Implement JwtAuthenticationConverter, override convert() to extract claims and create JwtAuthenticationToken with authorities from 'roles' or 'authorities' claim.

questions.text: What is the purpose of SecurityFilterChain bean in modern Spring Security configuration?
options: To define which security filters apply to which request paths (allowing multiple filter chains)
questions.explanation: SecurityFilterChain allows multiple filter chains for different request patterns. Example: /api/** uses stateless JWT chain, /admin/** uses different authentication.

questions.text: How do you prevent the 'Remember Me' token from being stolen and replayed?
options: Implement PersistentTokenBasedRememberMeServices with database-stored series and token values (validation on each request)
questions.explanation: PersistentTokenBasedRememberMeServices stores (series, token) pairs. On each auto-login, token is validated and new token generated. Stolen token becomes invalid after use.

questions.text: What is the vulnerability that Spring Security's HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) prevents?
options: SSL stripping attacks (forcing HTTPS by telling browser to always use HTTPS)
questions.explanation: HSTS adds Strict-Transport-Security header, instructing browser to always use HTTPS for the domain, preventing man-in-the-middle downgrade to HTTP.

questions.text: How do you configure Spring Security to return 401 instead of redirecting to login page for REST APIs?
options: Configure AuthenticationEntryPoint to send 401 response instead of redirect
Both B and C (disable formLogin + custom AuthenticationEntryPoint)
questions.explanation: For REST APIs, disable formLogin() to remove login page redirect, and configure custom AuthenticationEntryPoint that returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate header.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff' header?
options: Prevents MIME type sniffing, reducing XSS risk
questions.explanation: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff prevents browser from guessing MIME type when server declares Content-Type. Prevents drive-by download attacks and XSS via malformed responses.

questions.text: How do you implement brute force protection (account lockout) in Spring Security?
options: Implement custom AuthenticationFailureHandler that tracks failed attempts in cache/database and locks account after threshold
Spring Security has built-in brute force protection
questions.explanation: Implement AuthenticationFailureHandler to increment failed attempts counter (e.g., in Redis). When threshold reached, update UserDetails to lock account or add to blocklist.
